meta.note: Theoretical/methodological paper with Monte Carlo simulations and two empirical illustrations. Many quantitative claims rely on figures (small plots) whose exact plotted values are not transcribed in text; figure-read evidence is given lower confidence.

claims.text: The proposed semiparametric local projection (DR-NLO) estimator is √T-consistent and asymptotically normal.

location.quote:
The resulting estimator is √
T-consistent and asymptotically normal.

claims.text: The preliminary estimation of the nuisance functions has no effect on the first-order asymptotic distribution; the estimator is asymptotically equivalent to the infeasible oracle estimator.

location.quote:
does not affect the first-order asymptotic distribution of ˆθh: the estimator is asymptotically
equivalent to the infeasible oracle estimator that uses the true nuisance functions.

claims.predicate: first-order asymptotic distribution of θ̂_h equals that of oracle θ̃_h (remainder = op(T^{-1/2}))

claims.text: The doubly robust moment condition yields an estimator insensitive to misspecification of g_h provided α = α0, and insensitive to misspecification of α provided g_h = g0,h.

location.quote:
Hence, this moment condition yields an estimator that is insensitive to misspecification of
gh provided α = α0, and insensitive to misspecification of α provided gh = g0,h.

claims.predicate: E[ψ(...,g_h,α,θ0,h)]=0 for any g_h when α=α0 and for any α when g_h=g0,h
claims.scope: moment condition (6), via Theorem 5 of Chernozhukov et al. (2022)

claims.text: Proposition 4.1: under the stated conditions, ARF_h(δ) = E[g0,h(x_t+δ, z_{t-1}) - g0,h(x_t, z_{t-1})].

location.quote: θ0,h ≡ ARFh(δ) = E[g0,h(xt + δ, zt−1) − g0,h(xt, zt−1)] , (3)

claims.predicate: identification equation (3) holds under (1),(2) with mutually independent i.i.d.(0,Σ) shocks

claims.text: DR-NLO performs well for the ARF in models with nonlinear regressors: bias is small and close to zero across horizons and sample sizes; RMSE decreases with T; coverage falls below nominal 95% but improves with sample size.

location.quote:
The bias is small and close to zero
across horizons and sample sizes, indicating that the estimator is approximately unbiased.
RMSE decreases with T, as expected for a consistent estimator. Although coverage falls
below the nominal 95%, it improves with increasing sample size.

claims.predicate: median absolute bias small; RMSE decreasing in T; coverage < 0.95 but increasing in T per Figure 2
claims.scope: Figure 2, nonlinear regressor DGP, T∈{250,500,1000,2000}, 20,000 replications

claims.text: The DR-NLO estimator has smaller median bias than SD-LP at every horizon and for every sample size considered in the state-dependent design, and its bias decreases with T.

location.quote:
the semiparametric DR-NLO estimator has smaller median bias at every
horizon and for every sample size considered, and its bias decreases with T, as predicted

claims.predicate: median_bias(DR-NLO) < median_bias(SD-LP) for all h and all T in Figure 3
claims.scope: Figure 3, state-dependent model, expansion and recession, T∈{500,1000,2000}, δ=1, 30,000 replications

claims.text: The SD-LP estimator exhibits a systematic downward bias in both expansions and recessions that does not vanish as the sample size grows.

location.quote:
Figure 3 illustrates the systematic downward bias of the traditional state-dependent
LP (SD-LP) estimator in both expansions and recessions, which does not vanish as the
sample size grows.

claims.predicate: SD-LP median bias negative and non-vanishing as T increases per Figure 3

claims.text: SD-LP confidence intervals exhibit substantial undercoverage that worsens as T increases, while DR-NLO coverage remains closer to nominal 95% and improves with T.

location.quote:
The SD-LP intervals exhibit substantial undercov-
erage for all sample sizes, which worsens as T increases since the variance shrinks while the
bias persists. DR-NLO slightly undercovers as well, but its coverage remains closer to the
nominal 95% level and improves with T.

claims.predicate: coverage(SD-LP) decreasing in T and far below 0.95; coverage(DR-NLO) near 0.95 and increasing in T per Figure 5
claims.scope: Figure 5, state-dependent model, δ=1, nominal 95% intervals

claims.text: Headline inflation jumps by close to 0.05 percentage points (not annualized) in response to a one percentage point gasoline price shock, then declines quickly and is close to zero by horizon 2.

location.quote:
Both estimates indicate that headline inflation jumps by close
to 0.05 percentage points (not annualized) in response to a one percentage point gasoline
price shock, but the response declines quickly. By horizon 2, it is close to zero.

claims.predicate: impact response of headline inflation ≈ 0.05 pp for δ=1 pp, matching Figure 6 plotted value
claims.scope: Figure 6, headline inflation, δ=1, sample Jan 1974–Apr 2026

claims.text: Both linear LP and DR-NLO estimates of core inflation are marginally statistically significant at horizons 1 and 2; the core inflation response is an order of magnitude smaller than headline.

location.quote:
The
response of core inflation is an order of magnitude smaller and only slightly positive. Both
the linear LP and the DR-NLO estimate are marginally statistically significant at horizons 1
and 2.

claims.predicate: core inflation response statistically significant (CI excludes 0) at horizons 1 and 2 per Figure 6

claims.text: The historical average annualized inflation over the estimation period is 3.8%, i.e. 0.32% monthly.

location.quote:
exceeding its historical average of 3.8% (i.e., 0.32%
monthly) during the estimation period

claims.predicate: monthly_rate consistent with annualized 3.8% (0.32% monthly stated)

claims.text: At horizon 12, the linear LP estimate of the auto-sales response is only 28% of the DR-NLO response.

location.quote:
At horizon 12, the linear LP estimate shows a decline
in auto sales that is only 28% of the DR-NLO response.

claims.predicate: LP_horizon12 / DRNLO_horizon12 == 0.28 consistent with Figure 8 plotted values
claims.scope: Figure 8, motor vehicle sales, horizon 12, δ=1 s.d.

claims.text: The DR-NLO impact response of motor vehicle sales is positive but statistically insignificant, with no statistically significant response for the first three months followed by a persistent decline starting at horizon 3.

location.quote:
The DR-NLO impact response is positive but
statistically insignificant. There is no statistically significant response in sales for the first
three months, according to the DR-NLO estimator, followed by a persistent decline in auto

claims.predicate: DR-NLO sales response CI includes 0 at horizons 0-3, negative thereafter per Figure 8
claims.scope: Figure 8, motor vehicle sales, DR-NLO, δ=1 s.d.

claims.text: The linear LP estimate suggests a statistically significant increase in auto sales at horizons 0, 1 and 2.

location.quote:
The linear LP estimate, in contrast, suggests a statistically
significant but economically counterintuitive increase in auto sales at horizons 0, 1 and 2.

claims.predicate: LP sales response CI excludes 0 and positive at horizons 0,1,2 per Figure 8
claims.scope: Figure 8, motor vehicle sales, linear LP

claims.text: The structural shocks are drawn i.i.d. from a Student-t distribution with ν = 10 degrees of freedom rescaled to unit variance in the simulations.

location.quote:
each drawn i.i.d. from a Student-t distribution with ν = 10
degrees of freedom rescaled to unit variance, so that the size of the shock can be interpreted
as a one-standard-deviation shock.

claims.predicate: simulation DGP uses Student-t(ν=10) shocks rescaled to unit variance

claims.text: True impulse responses are computed by simulation using 5,000 counterfactual paths after a burn-in period of T0 = 500.

location.quote:
The true impulse responses are computed by simulation from the structural model using
5,000 counterfactual paths after a burn-in period of T0 = 500.

claims.predicate: n_paths == 5000; burn_in == 500

claims.text: The DR-NLO estimator uses K = 10 blocks for the state-dependent model and K = 20 blocks for the nonlinear regressor model.

location.quote:
The DR-NLO estimator uses
K = 10 (K = 20) contiguous blocks of equal length ⌊T/K⌋, as described in Section 5 for the
state-dependent (nonlinear regressor) model.

claims.predicate: K=10 (state-dependent), K=20 (nonlinear regressor)

claims.text: The state-dependent simulation parameters are ρx = 0.3, ψx = −0.1, βE = 2.5, βR = 3.5, γE = 0.9, γR = −0.1.

location.quote:
The parameters are
set to ρx = 0.3, ψx = −0.1, βE = 2.5, βR = 3.5,γE = 0.9, and γR = −0.1.

claims.predicate: parameter set {ρx=0.3, ψx=-0.1, βE=2.5, βR=3.5, γE=0.9, γR=-0.1}

claims.text: NLO cross-fitting partitions the index set into K ≥ 4 non-overlapping blocks of contiguous time indices, each of size T/K, with K kept fixed as T → ∞.

location.quote:
We partition
the index set {1, . . . , T} into K ≥ 4 non-overlapping blocks I1, . . . , IK of contiguous time
indices, each of size Tℓ ≡ T/K

claims.predicate: K ≥ 4 blocks, each size T/K, K fixed as T→∞

claims.text: In the sign-model simulation a one percentage point shock and the Student-t distribution make the population Riesz representer uniformly bounded, satisfying Assumption 2(i).

location.quote:
With δ = 1 and the use of a t-distribution for generating ε1t, the population Riesz representer α0(x) is
uniformly bounded, satisfying Assumption 2(i).

claims.predicate: α0(x) uniformly bounded for δ=1 with Student-t shocks => Assumption 2(i) satisfied
